walkthrough.title: Choose Your Heroes
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Marvel Champions set up. First, each player picks a hero to play. The core set comes with five heroes: Spider-Man, Captain Marvel, She-Hulk, Iron Man, and Black Panther. Each hero has a double-sided identity card with the hero on one side and their alter-ego on the other. Grab your hero's identity card and set it in front of you, alter-ego side face up.

walkthrough.title: Grab Your Hero Deck
walkthrough.text: Each hero comes with a set of fifteen unique hero cards. These cards have a special icon matching your hero and are only used by you. Find all fifteen of your hero-specific cards and set them aside. You will shuffle these into your player deck in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Player Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's build your full deck. Choose one of the four aspects: Aggression, Justice, Leadership, or Protection. Each aspect has its own colored cards. Combine your fifteen hero cards with aspect cards and basic cards until you have between forty and fifty cards total. You can include up to three copies of any non-unique card. For your first game, we recommend using one of the pre-built decks from the rulebook.

walkthrough.title: Set Your Hit Points
walkthrough.text: Look at the bottom of your identity card. You will see a hit point value printed there. Set your hit point tracker or dial to that number. This is how much damage your hero can take before being defeated. Keep your tracker where everyone can see it.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Shuffle your player deck and place it face down near your identity card. Now draw cards equal to your alter-ego hand size, which is printed on the alter-ego side of your identity card. This is usually six cards. Look at your hand, and if you do not like what you see, you get one mulligan. Set aside any cards you want to replace, draw that many new ones, then shuffle the discarded cards back into your deck.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside Your Nemesis and Obligation
walkthrough.text: Each hero has a nemesis mini-set and an obligation card. Find your obligation card and set it aside for now. We will shuffle it into the encounter deck shortly. Your nemesis set stays out of the game unless a special card brings it in. Place the nemesis set face down near your play area where it will not get mixed up with anything else.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Villain
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the villain. The core set includes three villains: Rhino, Klaw, and Ultron. Rhino is the easiest and best for learning. Find the villain's card set and place the Stage One villain card face up in the center of the table. Each villain has multiple stages that get progressively harder.

walkthrough.title: Set the Villain's Hit Points
walkthrough.text: Look at the villain card for the hit point value. It will say something like "per player." Multiply that number by the number of players at the table. For example, if Rhino Stage One says fourteen hit points per player and you have two players, set the villain's hit points to twenty-eight. Use a dial or damage tokens to track this.

walkthrough.title: Place the Main Scheme
walkthrough.text: Find the main scheme cards for your chosen villain. Place the Stage One A side face up near the villain card. The main scheme shows a threat threshold. If threat on the scheme ever reaches that number, the scheme advances to the next stage. Some schemes have starting threat instructions, so check the card and place any initial threat tokens as directed.

walkthrough.title: Build the Encounter Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's build the villain's encounter deck. Each villain scenario tells you which encounter sets to include. Gather those required sets, then choose one modular encounter set to add. The modular set adds variety, so feel free to pick whichever sounds fun. For your first game with Rhino, the Bomb Scare modular set is a good choice. Shuffle all of these cards together into one face-down encounter deck.

walkthrough.title: Add Obligation Cards
walkthrough.text: Remember those obligation cards we set aside? Now shuffle each player's obligation card into the encounter deck. These cards represent your hero's personal baggage. When one comes up during the villain phase, it creates problems specific to that hero. Give the encounter deck one more good shuffle after adding them.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Token Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the damage tokens, threat tokens, acceleration tokens, and status cards in a central supply where everyone can reach them. The status cards are Stunned, Confused, and Tough. You will use these throughout the game, so keep them organized and accessible.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the first player and give them the first player token. The first player takes their hero turn first each round. The token will pass clockwise at the end of each round so everyone gets a turn going first.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Setup Instructions
walkthrough.text: Almost ready. Check the villain card and the main scheme for any text that says "Setup." Some villains or schemes have special instructions that happen before the game begins, like placing extra threat or revealing encounter cards. Resolve those instructions now. Everyone starts in alter-ego form. Let's play!

summary.title: Resolve Setup Instructions

Resolve any setup instructions before play begins
All heroes start in alter-ego form

walkthrough.title: The Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Marvel Champions is played in rounds, and each round has two phases. First comes the player phase, where each player takes a complete hero turn in clockwise order. Then comes the villain phase, where the villain fights back against everyone. You keep playing rounds until either the heroes win by defeating the villain or the villain wins by completing the main scheme or knocking out all the heroes.

walkthrough.title: Your Hero Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your hero turn, you can do as many things as you want in any order. There is no strict sequence to follow. You can play cards from your hand, use abilities on cards already in play, trigger your hero's basic power, and flip between hero and alter-ego form. The only limit is your resources and your imagination. When you are done doing everything you want to do, your turn is over and the next player goes.

walkthrough.title: Paying for Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most important concept in the game: resources. Every card in your hand has a cost in the upper left corner. To pay for a card, you discard other cards from your hand. Look at the bottom left of each card you are discarding. You will see resource icons there. Each icon generates one resource. Some cards have double icons, generating two resources of that type. Wild icons count as any type. The key tension is that every card you spend for resources is a card you cannot play for its effect.

walkthrough.title: Playing Allies, Supports, and Upgrades
walkthrough.text: There are three types of cards that stay in play once you pay for them. Allies are other characters who fight alongside you. They have their own attack and thwart values and hit points. Supports go into your play area and provide ongoing abilities or resources. Upgrades attach to your hero and give you new powers or stat boosts. All three types stay in your play area until something removes them.

walkthrough.title: Playing Events
walkthrough.text: Events are one-shot cards. You pay their cost, resolve the effect printed on the card, and then discard them. Events can be powerful because they often deliver a big burst of damage, healing, or threat removal in a single action. Some events have specific timing requirements, like only being playable during the villain phase or when defending.

walkthrough.title: Using Basic Powers
walkthrough.text: Your hero has four basic powers printed on the identity card. In hero form, you can attack, thwart, or defend. In alter-ego form, you can recover. To use a basic power, you exhaust your identity card by turning it sideways. You can only use one basic power per turn since exhausting your card is required. Attack deals your ATK value as damage to the villain. Thwart removes your THW value of threat from a scheme. Recover heals your REC value of hit points.

walkthrough.title: Defending Basics
walkthrough.text: Defending is special because it happens during the villain phase, not your turn. When the villain attacks you, you may exhaust your hero or an ally to defend. If you defend with your hero, reduce the incoming damage by your DEF value. If an ally defends, they take the damage instead of you. If nobody defends, all the damage goes straight to your hero's hit points. This is called undefended damage, and it hurts.

walkthrough.title: Flipping Between Forms
walkthrough.text: Once per turn, you may flip your identity card between hero and alter-ego form. This is huge and it exhausts your identity card. In hero form, you can attack and thwart, but the villain attacks you during the villain phase. In alter-ego form, you can recover hit points, but the villain schemes instead, adding threat to the main scheme. You also draw an extra encounter card in alter-ego. Choosing when to flip is the core strategic decision in this game.

walkthrough.title: Using Allies
walkthrough.text: Allies are incredibly valuable. Each ally can attack or thwart using their printed values without exhausting your hero. The catch is that allies take one consequential damage every time they attack or thwart. This means allies wear down over time and eventually leave play. Allies can also defend against villain attacks, taking the full damage instead of your hero. A well-timed ally block can save your life.

walkthrough.title: The Villain Phase: Threat
walkthrough.text: Now let's talk about what the villain does. At the start of the villain phase, place threat on the main scheme. The base amount is one threat per player, plus any acceleration tokens in play. This threat steadily pushes the scheme toward its threshold. If the main scheme's threat ever reaches the threshold, the scheme advances to its next stage, and if the final stage completes, you lose the game.

walkthrough.title: The Villain Phase: Activation
walkthrough.text: Next, the villain activates against each player in turn order. What happens depends on your form. If you are in hero form, the villain attacks you. Draw a boost card from the encounter deck to add to the villain's attack value, then deal that much damage. If you are in alter-ego form, the villain schemes instead. Draw a boost card and add that many threat to the main scheme. This is why alter-ego form is risky — the villain piles on threat every round you stay hidden.

walkthrough.title: The Villain Phase: Encounter Cards
walkthrough.text: After the villain activates against everyone, each player draws one encounter card from the encounter deck. If you are in alter-ego form, you draw an additional encounter card, so two total. Encounter cards can be treacheries that resolve immediately, minions that engage you and must be dealt with, attachments that power up the villain, or side schemes that create new threat problems. Resolve each encounter card as you draw it.

walkthrough.title: Dealing with Minions
walkthrough.text: Minions are mini-villains that engage you personally. An engaged minion will attack you during the villain phase in addition to the villain's attack. You need to deal damage to minions just like the villain to defeat them. Allies can attack minions, and your hero can target them with attacks. Clearing minions quickly is important because they stack up damage fast if left unchecked.

walkthrough.title: Side Schemes
walkthrough.text: Side schemes are secondary schemes that enter play from the encounter deck. They start with a certain amount of threat and often have nasty ongoing effects. For example, a side scheme might prevent you from removing threat from the main scheme until the side scheme is cleared. You remove threat from side schemes by thwarting, just like the main scheme. Keeping side schemes under control is critical to winning.

walkthrough.title: Status Cards
walkthrough.text: Three status cards can affect heroes, villains, allies, and minions. Stunned means the next time that character would attack, they discard the Stun card instead and the attack does not happen. Confused means the next time that character would thwart or the villain would scheme, they discard the Confused card instead. Tough means the next time that character would take any amount of damage, they discard the Tough card and take zero damage instead. These are powerful effects.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Up and End of Round
walkthrough.text: At the end of each player's turn during the player phase, you draw back up to your hand size. In hero form, your hand size is usually five. In alter-ego form, it is usually six. This is a key benefit of alter-ego: you get one more card to work with next turn. After the entire villain phase completes, pass the first player token clockwise and begin a new round.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: Let's be clear about how the game ends. You win by reducing the villain's final stage to zero hit points. You lose if the main scheme's final stage reaches its threat threshold, or if all heroes are eliminated. If one hero is knocked out, the remaining heroes keep fighting. This is a cooperative game, so talk to your teammates, plan your moves together, and coordinate who attacks and who thwarts each round.

summary.title: The Round Structure
bullets: Each round: Player Phase (hero turns clockwise) → Villain Phase
Repeat rounds until win or loss condition is met

Cooperative — communicate and coordinate every round

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Marvel Champions! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several rounds of a two-player game against Rhino, the easiest villain in the core set. We will use Spider-Man and Captain Marvel as our heroes. Follow along with the moves, even if you are playing with different heroes or player counts. The concepts apply to every game.

walkthrough.title: The Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Both players have drawn their opening hands of six cards each since we start in alter-ego form. Take a look at your cards. You want a mix of cheap cards you can play right away and resource cards that help you pay for things. If your hand is full of expensive cards with no way to pay for them, use your mulligan. Set aside the clunky cards, draw replacements, and shuffle the discards back in.

walkthrough.title: Spider-Man's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Spider-Man goes first. Let's start by playing a cheap ally from our hand. Find a two-cost ally and discard two cards from your hand to pay for it. Place the ally card in your play area. Now we have a friend who can attack or thwart for us. Since we started in alter-ego form and want to flip to hero, let's flip our identity card to the Spider-Man side. This exhausts the card, so we cannot use a basic power this turn.

walkthrough.title: Why We Flipped Early
walkthrough.text: Flipping to hero form on turn one is important because it means the villain will attack us instead of scheming during the villain phase. If both players stay in alter-ego, the villain schemes against everyone and threat piles up dangerously fast. Having at least one player in hero form keeps the scheme pressure manageable.

walkthrough.title: Captain Marvel's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Now it is Captain Marvel's turn. Let's play a support card to build our resource engine. Captain Marvel's hero kit includes some great resource-generating cards. Pay the cost and put the support into play. Then let's also stay in alter-ego for one more turn since Spider-Man is already in hero form to take the villain's attack. We will draw up to six cards at end of turn, giving us more options next round.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Threat
walkthrough.text: Here is a classic beginner error. Both players have been focused on setting up their boards, playing allies and supports. But nobody has thwarted the main scheme yet. Let's check the threat. Rhino's scheme started with some threat, and the villain phase is about to add more. If we are not careful, the scheme could advance before we even get rolling. Always keep an eye on the threat counter.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Monitor Threat from Turn One
walkthrough.text: The number one cause of losing in Marvel Champions is letting threat spiral out of control while you focus on dealing damage. The villain adds threat every single round, and encounter cards add even more. Make it a habit to check the main scheme's threat before and after every villain phase. If threat is getting close to the threshold, someone needs to thwart immediately, even if it means delaying your setup.

walkthrough.title: First Villain Phase: Threat Placement
walkthrough.text: Now the villain phase begins. First, we place threat on the main scheme. With two players, that is two threat plus any acceleration tokens. Place two threat tokens on the scheme. Check the threshold. We are getting close. This is why monitoring threat matters.

walkthrough.title: First Villain Phase: Villain Activation
walkthrough.text: Next, Rhino activates against each player. Spider-Man is in hero form, so Rhino attacks Spider-Man. Flip the top card of the encounter deck as a boost card. Add the boost icons to Rhino's base attack value. That is the total damage. Spider-Man can exhaust an ally to block, or take the hit. Let's use our ally to defend. The ally takes the full damage instead of Spider-Man.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Taking Undefended Damage
walkthrough.text: Captain Marvel is in alter-ego form, so Rhino schemes against her instead of attacking. But here is the thing some players forget: you cannot defend against a scheme. The villain just adds threat. Captain Marvel also draws two encounter cards instead of one because she is in alter-ego. One of those encounter cards is a treachery that deals damage to her. Since she did not have a defender ready, all that damage goes straight to her hit points.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Alter-Ego Has Hidden Costs
walkthrough.text: Staying in alter-ego seems safe because the villain does not attack you. But the villain schemes instead, pushing threat toward the loss condition. And you draw an extra encounter card, which can be anything from minions to treacheries to more threat. The only time to stay in alter-ego is when you desperately need to recover hit points or when your teammate can handle the threat for a round.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Emergency Thwarting
walkthrough.text: Round two begins, and the main scheme is getting dangerously close to its threshold. Spider-Man needs to thwart this turn. Exhaust Spider-Man's identity card and remove threat from the main scheme equal to his THW value. That bought us some breathing room. Remember, you can also use allies to thwart. Every bit of threat removal counts.

walkthrough.title: Captain Marvel Flips to Hero
walkthrough.text: Captain Marvel has recovered some hit points in alter-ego form and it is time to join the fight. Flip the identity card to hero form. This exhausts the card, so no basic power this turn. But next turn, Captain Marvel can start attacking the villain or thwarting schemes. With both heroes now in hero form, threat generation slows down because the villain attacks instead of scheming.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Wasting Allies on Damage
walkthrough.text: Spider-Man has an ally with only one hit point remaining. The temptation is to use that ally to attack the villain for a couple of damage. But wait. The villain phase is about to happen, and Rhino will attack Spider-Man. If we save that ally to defend instead, they absorb the entire attack. Two damage to the villain versus potentially ten damage blocked? The ally is worth far more as a shield.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Allies Are Best as Blockers
walkthrough.text: One of the biggest beginner mistakes is using allies purely for their attack values. Allies are often more valuable as emergency defenders. When an ally blocks, they take the full damage from the villain's attack, no matter how much it is. A one-hit-point ally blocking a ten-damage attack is an incredible trade. Get in the habit of keeping at least one ally ready to defend before the villain phase.

walkthrough.title: Dealing with a Minion
walkthrough.text: During the encounter card step, Spider-Man reveals a minion. This minion engages Spider-Man and will attack every villain phase in addition to Rhino. We need to deal with this quickly. On our next turn, let's use an ally to attack the minion while Spider-Man focuses on thwarting. Captain Marvel can attack the villain. Dividing responsibilities is what teamwork looks like.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Minions
walkthrough.text: Some players leave minions alive because they want to focus all their damage on the villain. This is a trap. Each minion attacks you every single villain phase, piling on damage that drains your hit points and forces you into alter-ego to recover. One minion is manageable. Two or three minions is a death spiral. Clear minions quickly so you can focus on the real fight.

walkthrough.title: Coordinating the Attack
walkthrough.text: By round three, both heroes should have some upgrades and allies in play. Now we coordinate. Spider-Man uses his basic attack plus an ally attack to deal damage to the villain. Captain Marvel plays a big event card for burst damage. Together, we chunk off a significant portion of Rhino's hit points. This is the rhythm of the game: manage threat, clear minions, then pour damage into the villain when you have the opening.

walkthrough.title: The Villain Advances a Stage
walkthrough.text: We have dealt enough damage to knock Rhino's Stage One to zero hit points. But the fight is not over. Rhino advances to Stage Two with fresh hit points. The new stage may have higher stats or a nasty ability. This is normal and expected. Do not panic. Reset your strategy, check the new villain card for any changes, and keep fighting. Many new players think they failed when the villain advances, but this is how the game is designed.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: Now we need to deplete the villain's Stage Two hit points while keeping the scheme in check. This is the endgame. Prioritize dealing damage, but never let threat get out of control. Save your biggest events and strongest ally turns for when the villain is close to defeat. One more coordinated round of attacks and Rhino goes down. That is how you win Marvel Champions!

walkthrough.title: Before You Suit Up
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, status effects, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun, heroes!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the biggest beginner traps. First, ignoring threat on the main scheme while chasing damage on the villain. Second, staying in alter-ego too long and letting the villain scheme every round. Third, using allies for chip damage when they would be far more valuable blocking a big villain attack. Fourth, spending all your best cards as resources instead of playing them for their powerful effects. Fifth, trying to do everything alone instead of coordinating with teammates. Keep these in mind and you will avoid most early losses.

walkthrough.title: Resource Management
walkthrough.text: Every card in your hand is both a potential play and a potential resource, and this dual nature is the heart of Marvel Champions. Before you discard a card to pay for something, ask yourself: is this card worth more played or spent? Low-cost cards with mediocre effects make great resources. High-impact events and key upgrades should almost never be discarded for resources. A good rule of thumb is to identify the two best cards in your hand each turn and protect them. Spend everything else.

walkthrough.title: When to Flip Forms
walkthrough.text: The hero and alter-ego flip is the most important decision you make each turn. Flip to alter-ego when your hit points are below half and you have a teammate in hero form to manage threat. Flip to hero when the main scheme is getting close to its threshold or when you have enough hit points to survive the villain's attack. In a two-player game, try to stagger your flips so at least one hero is always in hero form. Never have both players in alter-ego at the same time unless you are absolutely sure the scheme can handle the extra threat.

walkthrough.title: Ally Strategy
walkthrough.text: Allies are the most versatile cards in the game. They can attack, thwart, and defend, all without exhausting your hero. The key to ally management is knowing when to use them aggressively and when to hold them back. Early game, use allies to help set up while your hero focuses on building upgrades. Mid-game, use them to handle minions and side schemes. Late game, save them for critical blocks against the villain's biggest attacks. Always keep at least one ally alive going into the villain phase so you have a blocker ready.

walkthrough.title: Threat Management
walkthrough.text: Think of threat as a timer counting down to your loss. Every round, the villain adds threat to the main scheme. Every encounter card has a chance to add more. If you only focus on damage, you will lose to the scheme before you can defeat the villain. A balanced approach is essential. Assign one player to be the primary thwarter and one to be the primary attacker. The Justice aspect is the best at threat removal, but every hero can thwart with their basic power. Check the scheme's threat level at the start of every turn.

walkthrough.title: Deck Building Basics
walkthrough.text: When building your deck, think about what your hero needs help with. Spider-Man has great defense, so pair him with Aggression or Justice to shore up his damage or threat removal. Iron Man needs time to build his suit, so Protection or Leadership can keep him alive during setup. Include enough resource cards to consistently pay for your key cards. A deck with too many expensive cards will feel clunky. Aim for a curve with plenty of one-cost and two-cost cards, a few three-cost cards, and only a couple of expensive powerhouses.

walkthrough.title: Teamwork and Communication
walkthrough.text: Marvel Champions is cooperative, so talk to your teammates. Before each player phase, quickly discuss the board state. How much threat is on the scheme? Does anyone have minions engaged? Who needs to recover? Who has a big damage event ready? Planning your turns together prevents wasted actions and missed opportunities. The best teams coordinate their flips so someone is always in hero form and their aspect choices so all roles are covered.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: Every turn, ask yourself three questions before taking any action. How close is the main scheme to advancing? How many hit points does the villain have left on the current stage? And how healthy are all the heroes? If threat is high, prioritize thwarting. If the villain is close to advancing a stage, push damage. If a hero is low on hit points, they might need to flip to alter-ego while a teammate covers. Getting comfortable reading the board and adjusting your plan is what separates good players from great ones.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore threat — #1 cause of losses
Don't stay in alter-ego too long — villain schemes + extra encounter cards
Don't waste allies on chip damage — save them to block big attacks
Don't discard your best cards as resources
Communicate with teammates — don't try to solo everything

summary.title: Resource Management
bullets: Every card = potential play OR resource — weigh each time
Identify 2 best cards per hand, protect them, spend the rest
Low-cost mediocre cards make the best resources
Never discard key upgrades or high-impact events for resources

summary.title: When to Flip Forms
bullets: Alter-ego when HP < half AND teammate can handle threat
Hero when scheme is high or HP can survive attacks
Stagger flips — never both in alter-ego simultaneously
Alter-ego = bigger hand but villain schemes + extra encounter card

summary.title: Ally Strategy
bullets: Early: allies help set up while hero builds upgrades
Mid: allies handle minions and side schemes
Late: save allies for critical villain blocks
Always keep 1 ally alive going into villain phase

summary.title: Threat Management
bullets: Threat = countdown timer to loss
Assign primary thwarter and primary attacker
Justice aspect is best for threat, but all heroes have basic THW
Check scheme threat at start of every turn

summary.title: Deck Building Basics
bullets: Choose aspect that shores up hero's weakness
Curve: lots of 1-2 cost, some 3 cost, few expensive cards
Include enough resource generators for consistency
Match aspect to team needs — cover all roles

summary.title: Teamwork and Communication
bullets: Discuss board state before each player phase
Coordinate form flips — always 1 hero in hero form
Plan aspect choices to cover ATK, THW, and defense
Don't waste actions duplicating what a teammate already handled

summary.title: Reading the Board State
bullets: 3 questions each turn: scheme threat? villain HP? hero health?
High threat → thwart priority
Villain near stage defeat → push damage
Hero low HP → flip to alter-ego while teammate covers

walkthrough.title: Aspect Synergies
walkthrough.text: Each aspect has a distinct playstyle that shapes your entire game. Aggression focuses on dealing maximum damage to the villain and minions. Justice specializes in threat removal and side scheme control. Leadership builds an army of allies that multiply your actions. Protection keeps heroes alive through defense boosts and damage prevention. Understanding your aspect's strengths lets you play into them rather than fighting against them. An Aggression player should not be spending turns thwarting when a Justice player is at the table.

walkthrough.title: Optimal Team Composition
walkthrough.text: In multiplayer, the ideal team covers all three pillars: damage, threat management, and survivability. A classic two-player setup is Aggression plus Justice or Leadership plus Protection. With three or four players, you can specialize more heavily since each player covers a narrower role. The worst team compositions double up on one role while ignoring another. Two Aggression players will deal massive damage but lose to the scheme. Two Justice players will control threat but never kill the villain. Balance is everything.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Sequencing
walkthrough.text: The order you play your upgrades matters enormously. Resource-generating upgrades should come down first because they make everything else cheaper. Hero stat boosts come second because they increase your basic power output for the rest of the game. Situational upgrades and tech pieces come last. Iron Man is the extreme example: his suit upgrades are mandatory before he becomes effective. Plan your first two turns around getting your most important upgrades into play, even if it means doing nothing else.

walkthrough.title: Economy of Actions
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think in terms of action economy. Every turn, you have a limited number of things you can do: one basic power, your ally activations, and whatever cards you can afford to play. The best plays are ones that accomplish two things at once. An event that deals damage and stuns the villain is worth more than two separate cards that do those things individually. Similarly, an ally that attacks a minion and then defends a villain attack next round is giving you two actions for one card slot. Always look for two-for-one opportunities.

walkthrough.title: When to Push Damage
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to go all-in on damage is a critical skill. The best windows for burst damage are when the main scheme is safely below its threshold, all minions are cleared, and you have multiple damage events in hand. If you can knock out a villain stage in one coordinated turn, that is far better than chipping away over three turns while the villain fights back. Communicate with your team, count the total damage you can deal, and if it is enough to advance the stage, unleash everything at once.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Encounter Deck
walkthrough.text: The encounter deck is not purely random. You know what modular set you included, and you know which obligation cards are in there. This information lets you anticipate what might come. If you know a villain-specific card adds a big burst of threat, keep extra thwart capacity in reserve. If the modular set includes tough minions, save damage for them. Advanced players also track which nasty cards have already been discarded. If the worst treachery already came up, you can breathe easier for a few rounds.

walkthrough.title: Solo Play Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Solo play changes the entire dynamic. You must handle threat and damage alone, which means Justice or a hero with strong native thwart is almost mandatory. Your form flip decisions are even more critical because there is no teammate to cover while you recover. Many solo players prioritize getting an ally into play early for flexibility. The villain's scheme threshold feels much tighter in solo because you cannot distribute responsibilities. Consider playing with two heroes if pure solo feels too punishing.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty Scaling and Expert Mode
walkthrough.text: Once you are comfortable with standard mode, Expert mode adds a significant challenge. You start on the villain's Stage Two, which usually has higher stats and nastier abilities. Expert mode also adds an additional encounter set with tougher cards. The key to Expert is tighter play: fewer wasted resources, better coordination, and more respect for threat. Start by mastering Rhino on Expert before moving to Klaw or Ultron. Heroic mode, which deals extra encounter cards each villain phase, is for players who want even more punishment.

summary.title: Aspect Synergies
bullets: Aggression = max damage, Justice = threat control
Leadership = ally army, Protection = survivability
Play into your aspect's strength, don't fight against it
Let teammates cover what your aspect lacks

summary.title: Optimal Team Composition
bullets: Cover 3 pillars: damage, threat management, survivability
2-player: Aggression+Justice or Leadership+Protection
3-4 players: specialize more per role
Worst teams double one role and ignore another

Later: situational upgrades and tech pieces
Iron Man example: suit upgrades are mandatory first

summary.title: Economy of Actions
bullets: Think in actions: 1 basic power + ally activations + card plays

Allies that attack then defend = 2 actions from 1 card
Always look for 2-for-1 opportunities

summary.title: When to Push Damage
bullets: Best window: low threat, no minions, damage events in hand
Coordinated one-turn kill of a stage > chipping over 3 turns
Count total team damage before committing
Communicate with team — burst together

summary.title: Managing the Encounter Deck

Keep thwart reserve for big threat burst cards
Track which nasty cards have already appeared
Worst treachery already discarded = safer for a few rounds

No teammate to cover form flips — flip decisions even more critical
Prioritize early ally for flexibility

entries.definition: The double-sided card representing your hero. One side shows the hero form with ATK, THW, and DEF values. The other side shows the alter-ego form with REC value. Flip between forms once per turn, which exhausts the card.

A character card that stays in play and can attack, thwart, or defend independently of your hero. Allies take one consequential damage each time they attack or thwart. They can block villain attacks by absorbing all damage.
A one-time-use card. Pay its resource cost, resolve the effect, then discard it. Events often provide burst damage, healing, or threat removal. Some events have timing restrictions.
A card that attaches to your hero and remains in play, providing stat boosts, new abilities, or passive effects. Upgrades persist until removed by a card effect.
A card placed in your play area that provides ongoing benefits such as resource generation, card draw, or special abilities. Supports are not attached to your hero.

A card whose primary purpose is generating resources when discarded. Cards like Energy, Genius, and Strength each generate a specific resource type. The Power of cards generate a wild resource plus a bonus effect.
An encounter card that resolves immediately when drawn. Treacheries apply harmful effects like dealing damage, adding threat, or imposing status cards, then are discarded.
An encounter card representing an enemy that engages a player. Minions have their own ATK and HP values. They attack the engaged player every villain phase and must be damaged to defeat.
An encounter card that attaches to the villain or a scheme, enhancing their stats or adding harmful effects. Attachments remain in play until a specific condition removes them.
A secondary scheme that enters play from the encounter deck with its own threat. Side schemes often impose penalties or block actions until cleared. Remove threat by thwarting.

A personal encounter card for each hero, shuffled into the encounter deck during setup. When drawn, it creates a problem specific to that hero's alter-ego life, such as dealing damage or adding threat.

During the villain's activation, the top card of the encounter deck is flipped as a boost card. The number of star icons in the lower right determines how much extra ATK or threat the villain gains. The card is then discarded without resolving its printed effect.
One of the four player aspects (red). Focuses on dealing damage to the villain and minions. Includes cards with high ATK bonuses, direct damage events, and combat-oriented allies.
One of the four player aspects (yellow). Specializes in threat removal from schemes. Includes high THW allies, threat removal events, and cards that interact with side schemes.
One of the four player aspects (blue). Focuses on playing and empowering allies. Includes ally-buffing upgrades, cost reduction for allies, and cards that let allies ready or act multiple times.
One of the four player aspects (green). Focuses on defending, preventing damage, and keeping heroes alive. Includes DEF boosts, damage prevention events, and healing cards.
Neutral cards with no aspect affiliation that can be included in any player deck. Includes generic allies, resources like Energy/Genius/Strength, and versatile events.
One side of the identity card. In hero form, you can use ATK (attack), THW (thwart), and DEF (defend) basic powers. The villain attacks you during the villain phase. Hand size is typically five.

The other side of the identity card. In alter-ego form, you can use REC (recover) to heal hit points. The villain schemes against you during the villain phase, and you draw an additional encounter card. Hand size is typically six.

Turn a card sideways to indicate it has been used this phase. Exhausted cards cannot be used again until they ready. Heroes exhaust to use basic powers or flip forms. Allies exhaust to attack, thwart, or defend.
Return an exhausted card to its upright position. Cards automatically ready at the start of your turn during the player phase. Some card effects can ready cards at other times.
When a minion enters play, it engages a specific player. An engaged minion attacks that player during the villain phase. Some effects can move engagement between players.
One point of damage dealt to an ally each time it attacks or thwarts. This is automatic and cannot be prevented. It represents the wear and tear of an ally participating in combat.
Damage from a villain or minion attack that is not blocked by a defender. All undefended damage goes directly to the hero's hit points. Avoiding undefended damage is critical for survival.
The number printed on a scheme card. If the total threat on that scheme reaches or exceeds this number, the scheme advances to its next stage. If the final stage advances, the players lose.
A token placed on the main scheme by certain card effects. Each acceleration token increases the amount of threat placed during the villain phase by one. Multiple acceleration tokens stack.
A status card. The next time the stunned character would attack, remove the Stun card instead and the attack does not occur. Can be applied to heroes, allies, villains, or minions.

A status card. The next time the confused character would thwart (or scheme, for villains), remove the Confused card instead and the action does not occur. Confusing the villain prevents one round of scheming.

A status card. The next time the character would take any amount of damage from a single source, remove the Tough card instead and prevent all of that damage. Extremely powerful for blocking big hits.

Each villain has multiple stages (usually two or three). When a stage's HP reaches zero, the villain advances to the next stage with fresh HP and often increased difficulty. Defeating the final stage wins the game.
The primary scheme the villain is advancing. Threat accumulates on it each round. If the final stage's threat threshold is reached, the villain wins. Players remove threat by thwarting.

A small set of cards specific to each hero that starts out of play. Certain encounter effects bring the nemesis into the game as a minion with its own side scheme and support cards, creating a personal challenge for that hero.

A small set of encounter cards that can be added to any villain scenario for variety. During setup, you choose which modular set to include. This changes the types of minions, treacheries, and side schemes you face.
The default difficulty. The villain starts on Stage 1, and the standard encounter set is included. Recommended for learning and casual play.
Increased difficulty. The villain starts on Stage 2 (skipping Stage 1), and the expert encounter set replaces the standard set. Requires tighter play and better deck construction.
The highest difficulty modifier. Each player draws one or more additional encounter cards per villain phase, greatly increasing the pressure. Can be combined with Expert for maximum challenge.
